In the early 1950s, Iran was a constitutional monarchy with a parliament. In 1951, Mohammad Mossadegh became prime minister. He pursued popular reforms, including the nationalization of the British-controlled oil industry, which gained widespread support among Iranians.

The key actions of the CIA during this period:

The CIA, together with British intelligence (MI6), carried out a secret operation called Operation Ajax to overthrow Mossadegh and restore power to the Shah.

CIA agents ran psychological operations and propaganda, including planting anti-Mossadegh stories in newspapers and manipulating public opinion.

3. Funding unrest and protests
The CIA financed and helped organize demonstrations and unrest in Tehran, using money and support to local groups, which destabilized the political situation.

4. Military and political pressure
Through contacts in the Iranian army, the CIA helped coordinate troop movements and military actions that led to Mossadegh’s removal on August 19, 1953. He was imprisoned and later put under house arrest.

After the coup, Shah Mohammad Reza Pahlavi was fully restored to power and ruled for decades with strong US backing.
